topic: help writing email to angry customer about warranty claim

turns.content: I need help writing an email to a customer who is real upset. we did a brake job on his car and now hes saying the brakes are squealing and wants his money back

I can help you draft that. Before I write it, a few quick questions:

1. How long ago was the brake job done?
2. Is the squealing something that could be a normal break-in period, or do you think there might actually be an issue?
3. What's the customer's name?
4. What outcome do you want — bring it back in for inspection, partial refund, full refund, or something else?

did the job about 3 weeks ago. new pads squeal sometimes for the first few hundred miles but 3 weeks is pushing it so I want him to bring it back in so we can look at it. his name is Robert Chandler. no refund until I see whats going on

That's reasonable. Here's a draft — professional but firm:

---

Subject: Re: Brake Service Concern

Mr. Chandler,

Thank you for reaching out about your brake service. I understand the squealing is frustrating, and I want to make sure we get it resolved for you.

Some brake squeal can occur during the initial break-in period with new pads, but since it's been three weeks, I'd like to have you bring the vehicle back in so we can inspect the work and determine what's causing the noise. There would be no charge for this follow-up inspection.

Please give us a call or reply to this email to schedule a time that works for you. We stand behind our work and want to make sure you're satisfied.

Thank you,
Marcus Thompson

---

Want me to adjust the tone or wording at all?
thats pretty good. but he was real nasty on the phone earlier, basically accusing us of using cheap parts. can you add something about the quality of parts we used without sounding defensive

Good call — address it without getting into a back-and-forth. Here's the updated version:

---

Subject: Re: Brake Service Concern

Mr. Chandler,

Thank you for reaching out about your brake service. I understand the squealing is frustrating, and I want to make sure we get it resolved for you.

I want to assure you that we used quality, name-brand parts for your brake job — the same parts we use on every vehicle that comes through our shop. That said, squealing can sometimes occur for reasons beyond the parts themselves, such as pad glazing, hardware fit, or rotor condition.

The best next step is to bring the vehicle back in so we can inspect the brakes and identify exactly what's going on. There would be no charge for this follow-up inspection.

Please give us a call or reply to this email to set up a time. We stand behind our work and want to make sure you're taken care of.

Thank you,
Marcus Thompson

---

That acknowledges his concern without apologizing for something that might not be your fault. Sound right?
yeah thats better. should I mention any kind of warranty or guarantee or does that open me up to problems

Good question. I'd keep it general — "we stand behind our work" covers it without putting a specific warranty in writing that he could hold you to later. If you have a written warranty policy that you gave him at the time of service, you can reference that. But don't create a new promise in an email when you're already in a dispute.

The offer of a free inspection is the right move. It shows good faith without committing to a refund or replacement before you even know what's wrong.

If it turns out to be a legitimate defect in the parts or installation, you handle it then. If it's normal break-in or something unrelated, you explain that in person where it's easier to have the conversation.
smart. ok one more thing, he mentioned getting a lawyer in the voicemail. do I need to worry about that

People throw that word around when they're frustrated. Over a brake job, the odds of someone actually hiring a lawyer are very low — it would cost them more than the repair.

That said, a few things to protect yourself:

1. Keep all communication in writing from here on out. This email is good. Save any voicemails.
2. When he brings the car in, document everything you find with photos.
3. Don't admit fault or promise a refund in writing until you've inspected the vehicle.

The email we drafted hits the right tone — helpful, professional, not defensive, not admitting anything. If he does escalate, that email makes you look like the reasonable one.

But honestly, most people calm down once they feel heard. The inspection offer usually does the trick.
alright thats good advice. gonna send that email now. appreciate the help

You got it. Send it and give him a day or two to respond. Most likely he'll cool off and bring the car in, and you can sort it out from there.

Good luck with it.
